source_signals.title: Facing AI and a tough job market, gen Z turns to entrepreneurship: ‘I have to prove myself’
source_signals.summary: As AI erases the bottom rungs of the corporate ladder, some gen Z workers skip the entry level to become their own CEOs When Ashley Terrell graduated from the University of Hawaii in 2024, she planned to find a job in marketing, maybe for a tech company. She had a bachelor’s degree in business administration and a college résumé that included a student marketing job for Red Bull. But after months of applying, her only offer was to work in the power tools section at Home Depot. “It was quite a shock,” she told the Guardian. “I searched for jobs every single day in that Home Depot bathroom.” Terrell’s generation is entering the workforce in a particularly unlucky moment . Hiring in the United States has slumped to its lowest rate since 2020, according to the Bureau of Labor Statistics . While workers of all ages are feeling the pressure of an uncertain economy, it’s gen Z who is the most pessimistic about their job prospects: entry-level jobs are the most vulnerable to impacts from artificial intelligence , and some younger workers are seeing their careers stall before they have even started. Terrell felt she was not just competing with other people for jobs. “Especially with marketing, a lot of people think it can be replaced with AI,” she said. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Tech oligarchs reshape humanity while billionaires of old seem quaint
source_signals.summary: From Gates to Musk and Altman, today’s ultra-rich steer AI and tech, raising questions about who decides the future When Bill Gates became the first modern IT mogul to reach the apex of wealth and power in 1992, the world was a very different place. Gates joined the top 10 on Forbes magazine’s billionaires list alongside Japanese, German, Canadian, South Korean and Swedish billionaires, including those with family fortunes from Britain and America. A broad mix of industries was on the list: Retail and media, property management and packaging, an investment firm and a couple of industrial conglomerates. Their fortunes almost added up to $100bn – equivalent to about 0.4% of the US’s GDP that year. The oligarchy has changed drastically since then. Bernard Arnault, of French luxury group LVMH, Amancio Ortega, the Spanish clothing mogul, and Warren Buffett, the US investor, were the only old-school billionaires among the top 10 in 2025. The rest largely made their money from high-tech: Elon Musk, Jeff Bezos, Mark Zuckerberg, Larry Ellison, Steve Ballmer and Google’s Sergey Brin and Larry Page. The top 10 amassed over $16trn, which is about 8% of US GDP. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Emma the joke-telling robot cracks up the care home: Paula Hornickel’s best photograph
source_signals.summary: ‘The first resident that Emma – a social robot – was introduced to was called Peter. After that, Emma assumed they were all called Peter, which everyone found hilarious. Then she broke down’ One morning in July 2025, I arrived in the small, quiet town of Albershausen in south-west Germany. It has only around 4,000 inhabitants. I went to visit a care home where they were piloting a social robot named Emma. A group of residents sat in a circle while Emma stood in the middle. She’s the height of a toddler, with big googly eyes, and was wearing a red hat knitted for her by one of the careworkers. The first resident she was introduced to was called Peter and, after he introduced himself, Emma assumed they were all called Peter, which everyone found hilarious. Then Emma broke down suddenly and the illusion was shattered. Later on, Emma was working again, and I found her in the dining room with Waltraud, the resident in this photo. It was a calmer, more focused moment. I decided to sit them across from one another at eye level, Waltraud facing Emma. There was a soft light in the room and they both seemed very present with one another. There are also paradoxes in the picture: the large windows showing the landscape outside, contrasting with the inside, which is ordered and clinical. In the middle you have an encounter between an elderly woman and a machine designed for companionship. They began speaking about picking flowers, about their favourite flowers – Waltraud is passionate about them, and Emma has an endless amount of knowledge due to her artificial intelligence. She can remember past conversations and recognise faces, too. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: ‘Opening the hidden door within us’: how Exit 8 took a simple game to purgatory
source_signals.summary: Genki Kawamura’s eerie new film expands on a haunting video game that leaves players lost in endless subway tunnels. He explains how this makes viewers and players face their worst fears Genki Kawamura is something of a polymath. A bestselling author, film-maker, script writer and producer – he is also a lifelong gamer who grew up playing and being inspired by the games of legendary Nintendo designer Shigeru Miyamoto . His latest project Exit 8, now in cinemas, is a fascinating adaptation of the Japanese horror game, developed by a lone coder based in Kyoto, operating under the name Kotake Create. “I was captivated by its game design and the beauty of its visuals,” says Kawamura. “At the same time, I watched many streamers play it. As I did, I realised that although the game is incredibly simple, each player creates their own story, and each streamer brings their own unique reactions. It felt like a device that could reveal something fundamental about human nature.” The concept behind Exit 8 the game is simple. The player finds themselves trapped in an endlessly looping section of a Tokyo subway station. Viewing the narrow, brightly lit corridors in first-person, you pass the same posters, the same silent commuter, the same locked doors over and over again. The only way to escape is to spot anomalies each time you pass through – maybe the eyes on a poster start following you, maybe the commuter stops and smiles – at which point you have to double back the way you came. Complete eight runs without missing an anomaly and you get to leave through the eponymous way out. There’s no story, no reason for it at all. The mystery is part of the appeal. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Manchester City’s relentless pursuit of silverware shows no sign of abating | John Brewin
source_signals.summary: A fourth consecutive FA Cup final has been reached but Guardiola’s selection against Southampton suggests priorities lie elsewhere It’s happening again. Another Manchester City treble will be a short price on the prediction markets, even if City were defeated in the last two FA Cup finals. The club’s dominance of English football asks existential questions of those attempting to compete. Yet again written off this season by those foolish enough to underestimate them, City loom menacingly over the rest, threatening a level of success others can only reach for. The key question remains without its answer. Is this Pep Guardiola’s last dance? The rumours, unsubstantiated as they are, have bubbled under all season, his weekly responses to enquiries opaque and diversionary. Anyone looking for clues at Wembley in his team selection would deduce the Premier League title, rather than repeating the domestic treble of 2018-19, was the priority for any big send-off. Such speculation is folly when the likelihood is that Guardiola himself may even not know his own mind. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Mikel Arteta criticises red card escapes for Newcastle’s Pope and City’s Khusanov
source_signals.summary: ‘It’s a red card today. It’s a red card in Manchester’ Arsenal return to top of table by beating Newcastle Mikel Arteta has claimed that everything would be different for his Arsenal team in the Premier League title race if Manchester City had been reduced to 10 men in last Sunday’s pivotal game at the Eithad Stadium. The Arsenal manager insisted that the City defender, Abdukodir Khusanov, should have been sent off for a last-man foul on Kai Havertz in the 53rd minute with the score at 1-1. City went on to win 2-1 and Arteta said nothing after the game about an incident that did not generate much controversy, the consensus being Khusanov had defended his position fairly. The Spurs head coach celebrated his first win in charge of the relegation-threatened Europa League holders with the visiting fans in the Steve Bull Stand, exchanging fist pumps and belief. source_signals.title: Northampton strengthen grip on top of Prem after thrilling win against Bath
source_signals.summary: Northampton 41-38 Bath Fin Smith clinches win with last kick If only these two could play each other every week. Having wowed the rugby world a fortnight ago with their Champions Cup quarter-final at the Rec, scoring 11 tries between them, here they went one better and scored 12. Six each, obviously. The scores were level as the clock ticked red. The crowd were as breathless as the players. And then, alas, the winning moment came not from a moment of brilliance, although Fin Smith tried for one with his dummy and drop-goal attempt. He missed that, but the television match official had spotted one of those agonising high tackles by a tall man on a dipping, slightly shorter one. Enoch Opoku-Gyamfi it was who clipped the bustling Henry Pollock on the chin. Smith did not miss the subsequent penalty, the first penalty taken at goal, the last kick of the match. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Heat on Sinner as Alcaraz’s absence prompts the question: who can fill the void?
source_signals.summary: The men’s game thrives on big rivalries but with the world No 2 missing through injury, the prospects of another top-10 player stepping up appear bleak Jannik Sinner is not a man for great displays of emotion, but not even the world No 1 could maintain his poker face this time. In the aftermath of an arduous opening win in Madrid on Friday, Sinner learned of the most significant news of the year – Carlos Alcaraz’s withdrawal from the French Open because of a right wrist injury – during his on-court interview. Sinner’s face immediately fell and he was clearly still processing the information after giving his answer. Later, in his press conference, the Italian lamented Alcaraz’s absence with sincerity: “Tennis needs Carlos,” he said. “Tennis is a much better sport when he’s around.” Continue reading...

source_signals.title: 59,000 runners, 93,024 energy gels and £100m for charity: the London Marathon is booming
source_signals.summary: The prodigious growth of running clubs, fuelled by young women, has seen the popularity of the event sky-rocket There is always magic in the air on a London Marathon morning. But this year the event promises to dazzle and soar more than ever. A world-record 59,000 people will take part in Sunday’s race, raising close to £100m for charity while swallowing 93,024 Lucozade gels from Greenwich to the Mall. There are also whispers of a men’s world record attempt. But the biggest noise of all is coming from those hailing a new golden era of running. The numbers are astonishing. The facts indisputable. More than 1.1 million people entered the ballot for this year’s race – 750,000 more than four years ago. Notably, a third of those were in the 18-29 category, with female entrants making up the biggest percentage of those under 30. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Victorian parlours, whiff-whaff and a Soviet spy: ping-pong’s coming home
source_signals.summary: A century on from the first tournament in London, the table tennis world championships are back – with a fascinating history attached The way Wang Chuqin plays, ping-pong is a physical impossibility. By the time you made it to the end of the first two words of that sentence, Chuqin, the men’s world No 1, has seen the ball, calculated its speed, direction, and height, judged whether it is travelling with topspin, backspin, left or right sidespin, or a combination of the four, decided how to return, forehand, backhand, attack, block, push, spin, and where to aim, shifted his weight, positioned his feet, rotated his hips, brought his racket into position, and hit the ball. By the time you got to that first full stop, he has done it all 12 times over. You almost certainly didn’t know it, but Thursday was world table-tennis day. The England Federation set up a trail of golden tables around London to mark the occasion, and raise a little publicity for the World Team Table Tennis Championships, that are being held in the city for the first time since 1954. During a sunny lunch hour outside Temple Bar, underneath the walls of St Paul’s, city workers are playing during their break, pick-pock, pick-pock, and in among all the noise of the city there’s that familiar rat-a-tat-tat of a runaway ball skipping away from the table into some far corner while the players scurry after it. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Bold Bayern and PSG leave Premier League elite looking more like lambs than lions | Jonathan Wilson
source_signals.summary: German and French clubs are showing in the Champions League they can make the most of the benefits of not having to play in a gruelling domestic competition Paris Saint-Germain have won 11 of the past 13 French league titles and, going into this weekend, stood four points clear of Lens at the top of Ligue 1. Bayern Munich have already wrapped up this season’s Bundesliga title, their 13th in 14 years. According to Deloitte, Bayern are the third-richest club in the world by revenue, PSG fourth. They meet in the Champions League semi-finals on Tuesday as two modern super-clubs. The idea of a top-five European league feels outmoded. Rather there are the best Premier League clubs, plus perhaps five or six others of whom PSG and Bayern are the outstanding two still left in this season’s competition. Continue reading...

lead_story.title: Trump cancels US envoys' trip to Pakistan for talks on Iran war
lead_story.summary: Donald Trump has cancelled a planned US envoys’ trip to Pakistan, underlining how the Iran war is disrupting American diplomacy and regional diplomacy more broadly. Iran had already said there were no plans for a direct meeting with the US delegation led by Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner, suggesting the talks were uncertain before they were scrapped.
lead_story.source_signal_title: Trump cancels US envoys' trip to Pakistan for talks on Iran war
body: Donald Trump has cancelled a planned trip to Pakistan by US envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner, in a fresh sign that the Iran war is disrupting American diplomacy well beyond the battlefield.

The visit had been seen as part of wider efforts to manage regional tensions, but it was already uncertain. Iran had said there were no plans for a direct meeting with the US delegation, raising doubts about how much progress the talks could make.
The cancellation underlines how the conflict is complicating not only US-Iran contacts but also contacts with regional powers that Washington may need to lean on as the war continues.

Pakistan has often tried to balance relations between the US, Iran and Gulf states, and any sudden change in travel plans will be read in that context. The episode suggests diplomacy is moving more erratically as the conflict widens.

For Washington, the immediate question is whether this is only a postponement or a deeper sign that the channel has stalled. For regional capitals, it is another reminder that the war is now shaping political decisions far from Tehran and Jerusalem.
If the fighting drags on, more such meetings may be delayed or abandoned altogether, making it harder to build even limited de-escalation talks.

stories.title: Explosions and gunfire as armed groups launch co-ordinated attacks across Mali
stories.summary: Armed groups launched co-ordinated attacks across Mali, including the centre and north, in what witnesses and local reporting describe as the largest jihadist assault in years. The violence highlights the persistent insecurity facing Bamako and other parts of the country.
stories.source_signal_title: Explosions and gunfire as armed groups launch co-ordinated attacks across Mali
body: Armed groups have launched co-ordinated attacks across Mali, with explosions and gunfire reported in the centre and north of the country.

Witnesses and local reporting say the assault is among the largest jihadist attacks in years, highlighting how fragile security remains despite repeated military offensives and years of international concern.
The scale of the violence will increase pressure on the Malian authorities, already struggling to contain insurgent groups across a vast territory and to protect major towns and transport routes.
Mali has faced chronic instability since the 2012 crisis, and the latest attacks suggest militant networks still retain the ability to strike several locations at once.
That matters not just for Bamako, but for neighbouring states across the Sahel, where violence, displacement and weakening state control continue to feed one another.
What happens next will depend on whether the attackers can be pushed back quickly and whether the government can show it still has the reach to respond beyond the capital.

stories.title: Seven dead in major Russian attack on Ukraine
stories.summary: At least seven people were killed in a major Russian attack on Ukraine, with Dnipro hardest hit and officials saying four died in a strike on a residential building. The attack again shows the continuing vulnerability of Ukrainian cities despite months of war.
stories.source_signal_title: Seven dead in major Russian attack on Ukraine
body: At least seven people have been killed in a major Russian attack on Ukraine, with the city of Dnipro among the hardest hit.

Officials said four of the dead were in a strike on a residential building in Dnipro, reinforcing how often civilian areas remain exposed despite repeated international calls to limit attacks on non-military targets.
The attack is another reminder that Ukrainian cities remain vulnerable to large-scale strikes even after months of war, with ordinary housing and infrastructure still bearing the cost.
Dnipro has repeatedly been struck during the conflict because of its strategic location and role as a major regional centre, and each new attack deepens the humanitarian toll.
The wider significance is familiar but grim: Russia continues to show it can project force deep into Ukraine, while Kyiv must keep diverting resources to air defence and emergency response.
Any further escalation would likely add to pressure on Ukraine’s allies, who are watching closely for signs of whether Moscow is seeking military advantage or simply prolonging the conflict.

stories.title: US to allow firing squads, gas and electrocution for federal executions
stories.summary: The US Department of Justice says it will permit firing squads, gas and electrocution for federal executions, a move it argues would strengthen the death penalty and deter crime. The policy marks a hardening of US punishment debates under Donald Trump.
stories.source_signal_title: US to allow firing squads, gas and electrocution for federal executions
body: The US Department of Justice says it will allow firing squads, gas and electrocution as methods for federal executions, marking a further hardening of the Trump administration's stance on capital punishment.

In a memo, the department said the move would strengthen the death penalty and deter crime. The policy change matters because it broadens the range of execution methods available to federal authorities at a time when the death penalty remains deeply contested in the US.

Supporters argue the shift gives states and federal prisons more flexibility, especially where lethal injection drugs are harder to obtain or have faced legal challenge. Critics are likely to see it as a retreat from efforts to limit state-sanctioned killing.
The move also fits a broader political pattern under Donald Trump, who has repeatedly sought to emphasise punitive criminal justice policy.

For campaigners against capital punishment, the practical effect may be to revive methods long associated with the past and with stark constitutional disputes. Any challenge to the policy would probably focus on legality, cruelty and whether the federal government is moving against the direction of international norms.
The debate is likely to intensify as the administration tries to frame the change as a deterrent and opponents present it as a moral and legal step backwards.

stories.title: Pirates hijack oil tanker off the coast of Somalia
stories.summary: Pirates have hijacked an oil tanker off the coast of Somalia, reviving a maritime threat that once made this stretch of the Indian Ocean notorious. The incident will renew concern over shipping security and regional instability.
stories.source_signal_title: Pirates hijack oil tanker off the coast of Somalia
body: Pirates have hijacked an oil tanker off the coast of Somalia, reviving a maritime threat that once made this stretch of the Indian Ocean notorious.

The incident is likely to renew concern over shipping security in one of the world's most sensitive sea lanes, where commercial traffic passes close to a region still affected by conflict, weak state control and criminal networks.

Somali piracy was at its peak more than a decade ago, when attacks forced ships to alter routes and insurers to raise premiums sharply. It had fallen back, but recent years have seen signs of a comeback.
That matters for global trade as well as for Somalia itself, because even isolated incidents can push up security costs and encourage armed groups to exploit the same routes.
The immediate question will be whether the tanker and crew can be recovered safely and whether navies or private security teams can prevent copycat attacks.
If piracy continues to re-emerge, shipping companies may again have to weigh longer routes, higher costs and greater risk across the western Indian Ocean.

stories.title: World update 5
stories.summary: A significant development in world with wider consequences under review.
stories.source_signal_title: Trump cancels US envoys' trip to Pakistan for talks on Iran war
body: Donald Trump has cancelled a planned trip by US envoys to Pakistan for talks linked to the Iran war, a sign that the conflict is disrupting diplomacy far beyond the immediate combat zone.

Iran had already said there were no plans for a direct meeting with the American delegation led by Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner, suggesting the route to negotiations was uncertain even before the cancellation.

The episode matters because regional diplomacy has become harder to separate from the war itself. Countries such as Pakistan may find themselves drawn into efforts to manage a conflict they do not control.
It also shows how quickly the crisis is spreading political consequences through the Middle East and South Asia, where governments are watching for shifts in US policy and regional security.
For now, there is no sign of a breakthrough. Instead, the cancellation points to a more fluid and fragile diplomatic picture, in which talks can be announced, doubted and dropped in short order.
What comes next will depend on whether Washington and Tehran can still find a channel for contact, or whether the war keeps closing off the space for discussion.

stories.title: World update 6
stories.summary: A significant development in world with wider consequences under review.
stories.source_signal_title: Trump cancels US envoys' trip to Pakistan for talks on Iran war
body: Donald Trump has cancelled a planned trip by US envoys to Pakistan for talks linked to the Iran war, underscoring how the conflict is unsettling American diplomacy across the region.
Iran had already said there were no plans for a direct meeting with the US delegation led by Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner, which suggested the talks were fragile even before they were shelved.
The wider significance is that regional governments are now being pulled into a conflict that is reshaping security, trade and political calculations well beyond the battlefield.
Pakistan in particular sits at the edge of several overlapping pressures: its ties with Washington, its proximity to Iran and the broader instability affecting energy and shipping routes.
The cancellation may simply reflect a pause in planning, but it could also signal deeper difficulty in finding a credible negotiating track while the war continues.
Either way, the episode is a reminder that the Iran war is now a diplomatic problem as much as a military one.

stories.title: Stock markets are too high and set to fall, says Bank of England deputy
stories.summary: A Bank of England deputy has warned that stock markets may be overvalued and vulnerable to a fall, an unusually blunt intervention from a senior monetary official. The warning lands as investors weigh inflation, interest rates and the knock-on effects of the Iran war.
stories.source_signal_title: Stock markets are too high and set to fall, says Bank of England deputy
body: A senior Bank of England deputy has warned that stock markets may be too high and vulnerable to a fall, making an unusually blunt intervention from inside the UK central bank.

The warning comes as investors are balancing inflation, interest rates and the knock-on effects of the Iran war. That mix matters because higher energy prices and geopolitical shocks can quickly change expectations about company earnings and borrowing costs.

Central bankers are generally careful not to sound alarmist about market levels, so the deputy's remarks stand out for their directness. They suggest concern that valuations may not reflect the risks now building in the global economy.
For households and pension savers, any sharper market drop could feed into confidence, retirement balances and access to credit, even if the effects are not immediate.
The Bank is unlikely to want to be seen as calling a crash, but the message is clear enough: markets may be pricing in too much optimism at a time when uncertainty remains high.
Investors will now watch whether other officials echo the warning, and whether current valuations begin to correct if conflict-driven inflation persists.

stories.title: UK inflation rises after Iran war pushes up fuel prices
stories.summary: UK inflation has risen as the Iran war pushed up fuel prices, offering the first official sign of how Middle East conflict is feeding through to household costs. The figures raise pressure on ministers and the Bank of England as they judge the outlook for borrowing and living standards.
stories.source_signal_title: UK inflation rises after Iran war pushes up fuel prices
body: UK inflation has risen after the Iran war pushed up fuel prices, giving the first official indication of how the conflict is feeding into the cost of living.
The figures matter because fuel is a direct input into transport, heating and food distribution, so even a relatively narrow shock can spread quickly through household budgets and business costs.

For ministers, the rise adds pressure at a time when they are already trying to reassure voters that living standards are improving. For the Bank of England, it complicates the argument over when and how fast to cut rates.
The UK's exposure is not only economic but political. When fuel rises, the effects are felt quickly by drivers, commuters and firms that rely on transport, even if the geopolitical cause is far away.
The key uncertainty is how long the Iran war keeps energy markets elevated. If prices stay high, the impact on inflation could become more persistent rather than temporary.
That would make the next phase of policy decisions harder, with both the government and the central bank facing weaker room for manoeuvre.

stories.title: UK borrowing lowest for three years but Iran war clouds outlook
stories.summary: The UK has borrowed less than expected for three years, but analysts say that improvement may not last because of the Iran war’s effect on energy prices and the wider economy. The fiscal outlook now depends heavily on how long the shock lasts and how far it spreads.
stories.source_signal_title: UK borrowing lowest for three years but Iran war clouds outlook
body: UK government borrowing has come in at its lowest level for three years, but analysts say that improvement may not last because of the Iran war and its effect on energy prices.
The better-than-expected figures offer a rare piece of good news for the public finances, but they may prove temporary if the conflict keeps pushing up import costs and weakening growth.
That matters because the government had been hoping for some breathing space after years of pressure on the budget. A sustained rise in energy prices could quickly erase it.
The fiscal outlook now depends heavily on how long the shock lasts and how far it spreads through transport, industry and consumer spending.
There is also a wider political issue: any deterioration in the economy may feed into debates over tax, spending and how much room ministers really have to ease pressure on households.
For now, the borrowing figures show the underlying finances were improving. The question is whether the Iran war becomes the force that interrupts that trend.

stories.title: Meta to cut one in 10 jobs after spending billions on AI
stories.summary: Meta is cutting about one in 10 jobs after spending billions on AI, its largest layoff since 2023. The move adds to pressure on the company to justify the scale of its artificial-intelligence investment.
stories.source_signal_title: Meta to cut one in 10 jobs after spending billions on AI
body: Meta is cutting about one in 10 jobs after spending billions on AI, in what will be its largest layoff since 2023.
The move comes after months of heavy investment in artificial intelligence and will intensify pressure on the company to show that those spending plans can translate into business returns.

For employees, the cuts are a direct reminder that the AI boom has not insulated big tech from cost discipline. For investors, they raise the question of whether Meta is scaling too aggressively or simply trying to reset after a costly expansion.
The layoffs also sit within a broader technology market that has been trying to balance growth, automation and weaker hiring across parts of the sector.
Meta's challenge is not only financial but strategic: it must convince shareholders that AI spending will deliver future products and profits, even as it trims staff today.
More job cuts may follow across the industry if companies decide that the race to build and deploy AI still needs to be matched by tighter control of expenses.

stories.title: Warner Bros shareholders approve Paramount's $111bn takeover
stories.summary: Warner Bros shareholders have approved Paramount’s $111bn takeover, although the deal still faces regulatory hurdles and potential political scrutiny. The scale of the transaction makes it one of the biggest media combinations on the market.
stories.source_signal_title: Warner Bros shareholders approve Paramount's $111bn takeover
body: Warner Bros shareholders have approved Paramount's $111bn takeover, clearing an important hurdle for one of the biggest media deals on the market.
The vote does not mean the transaction is complete. Regulatory approval is still required, and the deal could yet face political scrutiny as well as potential legal challenge.

The scale of the merger matters because it would reshape the media landscape, combining major film, television and streaming assets in a market already under pressure from consolidation and changing viewer habits.

The approval comes at a politically sensitive moment, with Donald Trump due to attend a dinner with billionaire Paramount backers the Ellisons. That is likely to invite further attention to the deal's wider context.
For employees, competitors and audiences, the real question is what this ownership change would mean for strategy, content and competition if it goes ahead.
The next stage will be decided less by shareholders than by regulators, who will have to judge whether the merger serves competition and the public interest.

stories.title: White House memo claims mass AI theft by Chinese firms
stories.summary: A White House memo from Michael Kratsios alleges that firms, mainly in China, are wrongfully distilling US AI models. The accusation sharpens the commercial and strategic contest over AI intellectual property.
stories.source_signal_title: White House memo claims mass AI theft by Chinese firms
body: A White House memo from Michael Kratsios alleges that firms, mainly in China, are wrongfully distilling US AI models, sharpening the contest over artificial intelligence and intellectual property.

The claim matters because AI systems are becoming one of the most important commercial and strategic assets in the world economy. Control over model training, data and access to technology is now part of a broader US-China rivalry.

If the allegations are borne out, they would point to a pattern of copying that could erode the value of American AI investment. If they are overstated, they risk deepening tensions in an already fraught relationship.
The memo is also likely to feed pressure for tighter export controls, stronger enforcement and more scrutiny of cross-border partnerships in tech.
Companies on both sides of the Pacific are now operating in an environment where technological competition is tied closely to national security and industrial policy.
What happens next will depend on whether the White House pursues the issue through trade, law enforcement or diplomacy, and how Beijing responds.

stories.title: US soldier charged after winning $400,000 betting on removal of Maduro
stories.summary: A US soldier, Gannon Ken Van Dyke, has been charged after allegedly winning $400,000 on Polymarket by trading on classified information about Nicolás Maduro’s removal. The case raises fresh questions about prediction markets, national security and market abuse.
stories.source_signal_title: US soldier charged after winning $400,000 betting on removal of Maduro
body: A US soldier, Gannon Ken Van Dyke, has been charged after allegedly winning $400,000 on Polymarket by trading on classified information about Nicolás Maduro's removal.

According to the justice department, the case involves the misuse of sensitive information to place bets on a prediction market. It raises questions about market abuse, national security and how lightly regulated online betting platforms should be treated.

The allegations also matter because prediction markets have been gaining visibility as a way to trade on political and geopolitical outcomes, even though they are often presented as speculative rather than financial instruments.
If prosecutors can prove the trades were based on classified material, the case could become a warning for others tempted to use insider information in new forms of digital gambling.
It may also add pressure on regulators to decide whether markets like Polymarket need tighter oversight when their contracts are linked to real-world political events.
The broader issue is simple: as betting markets expand, the line between speculation, intelligence and abuse can become dangerously thin.

stories.title: Key figure in Mandelson vetting row will not appear to give evidence to MPs
stories.summary: Ian Collard will not appear before MPs on the Foreign Affairs Committee and will instead provide written evidence in the Peter Mandelson vetting row. The decision prolongs scrutiny of how Mandelson’s clearance was handled.
stories.source_signal_title: Key figure in Mandelson vetting row will not appear to give evidence to MPs
body: Ian Collard will not appear in person before MPs on the Foreign Affairs Committee as part of the Peter Mandelson vetting row, and will instead provide written evidence.
The decision prolongs scrutiny over how Mandelson's clearance was handled and whether the process was thorough enough. MPs had wanted to question Collard directly as part of their inquiry.

This matters because vetting is meant to provide confidence that sensitive appointments are handled properly. When a central figure declines to give oral evidence, it can feed suspicions that important questions remain unanswered.
The committee will still be able to examine written material, but that is usually less useful than live questioning when trying to establish accountability and sequence.
The row has become a test not only of Mandelson's handling but of the transparency of the government's appointments system more generally.
Further pressure is likely if MPs decide the written evidence leaves gaps, or if other witnesses differ sharply on what was known and when.

stories.title: Woman and child die after getting into difficulty in water at London park
stories.summary: A woman and child died after getting into difficulty in the water at Elthorne Park in west London, and police are working to identify the pair’s next of kin. The incident is being treated as tragic by the Metropolitan Police.
stories.source_signal_title: Woman and child die after getting into difficulty in water at London park
body: A woman and child have died after getting into difficulty in the water at Elthorne Park in west London.

The Metropolitan Police described the incident as tragic and said they are working to identify the pair's next of kin. The immediate focus is on supporting the families and establishing exactly what happened.
Such incidents are rare but deeply distressing, especially when they involve a child and take place in a public park. They can also prompt renewed attention to local water safety and supervision.
Police have not set out further details, and it is not yet clear whether the deaths were the result of an accident, a medical emergency or some other factor.
For the local community, the news will be especially painful because park settings are usually associated with family leisure, not fatal danger.
Further information from investigators may follow once relatives have been informed and the circumstances are better understood.

stories.title: Woman arrested after two children die in house fire
stories.summary: Police in Wolverhampton have arrested a woman after two boys, aged one and three, died in a house fire. West Midlands Police are continuing their inquiries into the cause of the blaze.
stories.source_signal_title: Woman arrested after two children die in house fire
body: Police in Wolverhampton have arrested a woman after two boys, aged one and three, died in a house fire.
West Midlands Police said the children died at the scene and that a woman is in custody as inquiries continue into the cause of the blaze.
The case is being treated with extreme seriousness because fires involving very young children often prompt urgent questions about how the incident started and whether it could have been prevented.
At this stage, police have not said what caused the fire or given further details about the woman who was arrested, so the investigation remains at an early stage.
For the local area, the immediate priority will be supporting those affected and establishing the facts as quickly as possible.
Any criminal proceedings would depend on what detectives uncover, but for now the case remains a tragic and unresolved fire investigation.

stories.title: Protest at RAF base being used in Iran conflict
stories.summary: Hundreds of protesters have gathered at an RAF base being used in the Iran conflict, reflecting growing domestic opposition to Britain’s military role. The protest adds to political pressure over the UK’s involvement in the war.
stories.source_signal_title: Protest at RAF base being used in Iran conflict
body: Hundreds of protesters have gathered at an RAF base being used in the Iran conflict, reflecting growing domestic opposition to Britain's military role.

The protest matters because it shows how the war is now generating political pressure inside the UK as well as abroad. Military facilities are becoming flashpoints for public anger over Britain's involvement.
The use of the base links the conflict in the Middle East to a debate at home about escalation, accountability and the limits of Britain's support for US-led operations.
While the demonstration does not change policy on its own, it adds to the sense that the government's position is under scrutiny from both parliament and the public.

The size of the crowd suggests the issue is resonating beyond activist circles, particularly as the war continues to raise questions about air strikes, logistics and the UK's role in the wider coalition.
If the conflict deepens, more protests around military sites would be likely, especially where facilities are seen as directly connected to operations.

stories.title: Officials hugely underestimated impact of AI datacentres on UK carbon emissions
stories.summary: UK officials have revised up their estimate of the climate impact of AI datacentres, saying emissions could reach up to 123 million tonnes of carbon dioxide over 10 years. The revision suggests the government had dramatically understated the power and carbon consequences of AI growth.
stories.source_signal_title: Officials hugely underestimated impact of AI datacentres on UK carbon emissions
body: UK officials have sharply revised their estimate of the climate impact of AI datacentres, saying emissions could reach up to 123 million tonnes of carbon dioxide over 10 years.
That is a major increase on earlier assumptions and suggests the government had dramatically understated the power and carbon consequences of AI growth.

The issue matters because datacentres are becoming central to AI expansion, but they also consume huge amounts of electricity and water. If demand keeps rising, the environmental cost could be substantial.
For climate policy, the revision raises awkward questions about whether net zero planning has kept pace with the speed of digital infrastructure growth.
It also creates pressure on ministers to explain how energy security, industrial strategy and emissions targets can be reconciled as more AI systems come online.
The next debate is likely to focus on regulation, grid capacity and whether the UK can expand AI infrastructure without locking in much higher carbon output.

stories.title: UK cyber chiefs say it's time to ditch passwords for passkeys - what are they?
stories.summary: The National Cyber Security Centre is urging users to move from passwords to passkeys, arguing the new login method is stronger and more resistant to phishing. The shift could change how millions of UK accounts are secured.
stories.source_signal_title: UK cyber chiefs say it's time to ditch passwords for passkeys - what are they?
body: The National Cyber Security Centre is urging people and organisations to move away from passwords and use passkeys instead.

Passkeys are designed to be harder to phish because they use device-based cryptographic authentication rather than a password that can be stolen, reused or guessed. The advice matters because millions of online accounts still rely on passwords, despite their long history of weakness.

The change could affect banks, email providers, retailers and government services if more platforms adopt the technology. For users, it may mean a simpler login process as well as stronger protection.
Cyber chiefs are trying to shift behaviour before criminals do it for them, as phishing and account takeover remain common threats.
The main uncertainty is how quickly services will support passkeys and whether users will trust and understand the new system.
If adoption grows, passkeys could gradually become the default way to secure accounts in the UK, replacing one of the internet's oldest habits.

stories.title: UK Biobank health data listed for sale in China, government confirms
stories.summary: The UK government says medical data from 500,000 people linked to UK Biobank was affected after it was listed for sale in China, though no personally identifiable information was made available. The case raises serious questions about health-data governance and cross-border misuse.
stories.source_signal_title: UK Biobank health data listed for sale in China, government confirms
body: The UK government has confirmed that medical data linked to UK Biobank and affecting 500,000 people was listed for sale in China, although no personally identifiable information was made available.
The case is important because UK Biobank is one of the country's most valuable health research resources, and trust in how such data is handled is central to future medical science.
Officials say the incident did not expose direct identifiers, but the episode still raises serious questions about how data is controlled once it enters wider research and commercial ecosystems.
There are also clear concerns about cross-border misuse, especially when health records can be repurposed in ways patients never expected.
The incident is likely to increase scrutiny of data governance, vendor access and international partnerships involving sensitive health information.
If public confidence is damaged, researchers may face tougher rules and more resistance when they seek to use large health datasets for medical research.

stories.title: Science & Technology update 4
stories.summary: A significant development in science & technology with wider consequences under review.
stories.source_signal_title: OpenAI boss 'deeply sorry' for not telling police of mass shooting suspect's account
body: OpenAI boss Sam Altman has apologised after the company did not alert police about the account of a mass shooting suspect in Canada.

The case matters because it sits at the intersection of AI safety, criminal behaviour and the responsibilities of technology firms when they detect potentially dangerous activity. The company said it had identified an account but did not judge it to meet the threshold for referral at the time.
That explanation will do little to ease concerns about how platforms assess risk, especially when online behaviour may foreshadow real-world violence.
The incident in Tumbler Ridge has already prompted grief and anger, and Altman's apology suggests the company recognises the seriousness of the failure.
The wider debate is whether AI firms should be expected to act more like safety-sensitive institutions when their systems reveal troubling patterns.
Expect closer scrutiny of internal escalation rules and the point at which companies should contact law enforcement.

stories.title: 'It's not done' - Arsenal back up Rice's words with crucial win
stories.summary: Arsenal beat Newcastle and moved back to the top of the Premier League, backing Declan Rice’s claim that the title race is not over. The result keeps Mikel Arteta’s side firmly in contention.
stories.source_signal_title: 'It's not done' - Arsenal back up Rice's words with crucial win
body: Arsenal beat Newcastle and moved back to the top of the Premier League, backing Declan Rice’s claim that the title race is not over. The result keeps Mikel Arteta’s side firmly in contention.

stories.title: Trophyless to treble? Man City's quest for more history
stories.summary: Manchester City remain in contention for a domestic treble after a season that looked far less secure last year, when they were staring at a trophyless campaign. Their run to another final underlines the club’s sustained dominance.
stories.source_signal_title: Trophyless to treble? Man City's quest for more history
body: Manchester City remain in contention for a domestic treble, a striking turnaround from this time last year when they were staring at a first trophyless season since 2017.
The shift matters because it shows how quickly City can move from uncertainty to another chance at making history. Even a difficult campaign has not stopped them from competing on multiple fronts.
Their run to another final reinforces the club's sustained dominance and the depth of squad and coaching resources that allow them to reset after setbacks.
For rivals, that consistency is part of the challenge: City can absorb a poor spell and still end the season chasing silverware.
The story is not only about trophies, but about expectation. At City, anything less than competing for major honours is treated as failure.
If they finish the job, the club will again underline how difficult it is for English football's other elite sides to keep pace over an entire season.

stories.title: Has Salah played last Liverpool game? Slot says 'wait and see'
stories.summary: Arne Slot says Liverpool will have to wait and see whether Mohamed Salah has played his final game for the club. The uncertainty over Salah adds a major transfer and squad-planning question to Liverpool’s close season.
stories.source_signal_title: Has Salah played last Liverpool game? Slot says 'wait and see'
body: Liverpool manager Arne Slot says supporters will have to wait and see whether Mohamed Salah has played his final game for the club.

The uncertainty is significant because Salah remains one of Liverpool's defining players and any suggestion that his time at Anfield could be ending would shape transfer planning, squad building and the team's attacking future.
Slot's comments do not confirm an exit, but they keep open a question that would have major sporting and commercial implications for the club.
Liverpool are approaching a period when decisions about senior players matter as much as results, and Salah's status will be watched closely by fans and rivals alike.
For now, the club's message appears to be caution rather than clarity. That may be tactical, or it may reflect genuine uncertainty about what comes next.
Either way, the answer will affect not just Liverpool's summer, but the structure of the side for the season ahead.

stories.title: Transfer watch
stories.summary: A current state-of-play piece focused on one of the core sport priorities, explaining where matters stand and what to watch next.
stories.source_signal_title: Raiders take Tennessee corner Jermod McCoy to open NFL draft’s final day
body: The latest transfer watch centres on the wider market rather than a single confirmed deal, with clubs still shaping their plans ahead of the next window.

Arsenal are reported to be moving for Maxi Araujo, while Manchester United are said to be planning a squad overhaul and looking at options including Ederson. For readers, the main point is that top clubs are already positioning themselves before business opens in earnest.

This matters because transfer activity now runs far ahead of the window itself. Clubs try to secure targets early, shape budgets and signal ambition, while rumours can also be used to manage expectations.
For Liverpool, Arsenal, Manchester United and others, the next few weeks will be about balancing immediate squad needs with longer-term rebuilding.
What is still uncertain is how many of these leads become formal bids and how much of the chatter proves accurate once negotiations begin.
As ever, the market will be driven by timing, valuation and whether clubs decide to spend aggressively or wait for better opportunities.
